_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: He proved a country could become one thing — then spent a century drowning in zeros proving it again.

museum_objects.title: 1861 5 Lire Coin (Florence Mint)
museum_objects.description: One of the first coins struck under the newly unified Kingdom of Italy's monetary system.
museum_objects.significance: Before this coin, 'Italy' was a collection of separate kingdoms with separate money. This coin is the first proof that unification was more than a map — it was a currency too.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the first coins made after Italy's many separate kingdoms unified into one country in 1861.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

display_alternatives.title: Catalogue of the valuable collection of ... Oriental, medieval and modern coins and medals, ... formed by the late Baron S. de Chaudoir, of St. Petersburg (IA catalogueofvalua00slei 8).pdf

museum_objects.title: Allied Military Currency (Italy, 1943)
museum_objects.description: Emergency currency issued by Allied forces in Italy during World War II occupation.
museum_objects.significance: For a moment, Matteo wasn't even printed by his own country — a stark reminder of how fragile national identity can be during war.
museum_objects.plain_language: Emergency wartime money printed by Allied forces occupying Italy during World War II, not by Italy itself.
museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Garibaldi's Red Shirt
museum_objects.description: An original red shirt associated with Giuseppe Garibaldi and his 'Redshirts' who fought for Italian unification.
museum_objects.significance: This shirt belongs to the man who made Matteo possible — Garibaldi's unification campaign is the reason there was ever a single lira to unify at all.
museum_objects.plain_language: An actual red shirt worn by Giuseppe Garibaldi, the revolutionary leader who helped unify Italy and made a single Italian currency possible.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

display_alternatives.title: Musée Masséna Nice - Giuseppe Garibaldi Portrait.jpg

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: The image shows a textile that is not clearly a shirt, and its association with Garibaldi is not evident from the image alone.

museum_objects.title: Italian Hyperinflation-Era Banknotes (1970s-1990s)
museum_objects.description: High-denomination lira notes (100,000 and 500,000 lire) reflecting decades of inflation before the euro's adoption.
museum_objects.significance: By the end, Matteo needed six zeros just to buy a cup of coffee — proof of how much trust can erode, one small devaluation at a time.
museum_objects.museum: Referenced via Banca d'Italia numismatic archive
museum_objects.plain_language: Italian banknotes worth hundreds of thousands of lire, showing how much the currency's value shrank over decades.
museum_objects.institution: Referenced via Banca d'Italia numismatic archive

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Museo del Risorgimento
museum_objects.description: A museum in Turin dedicated to the history of Italian unification, the political movement that created Matteo's country.
museum_objects.significance: Everything Matteo represents — one currency, one nation — is documented here in the city where the movement to unify Italy began.
museum_objects.plain_language: A museum in Turin entirely dedicated to the movement that unified Italy and made a single Italian currency possible.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

world_map.map_description: Matteo's lira was born from Italian unification in 1861, minted first in Florence and Turin, and lived through wars, hyperinflation, and its final retirement for the euro in 2002.

nodes.label: Rome (Museo della Zecca)
nodes.description: Site of Italy's modern mint museum, documenting the full history of lira coinage.

nodes.label: Turin (Museo del Risorgimento)
nodes.description: Birthplace of the Italian unification movement that created the need for a single national currency.

nodes.label: Florence Mint
nodes.description: One of the earliest mints to strike coins under the newly unified Kingdom of Italy.

nodes.label: Marsala, Sicily
nodes.description: Where Garibaldi landed with his Redshirts in 1860, launching the campaign that led to Italian unification.

nodes.label: Frankfurt (ECB)
nodes.description: Where the lira's 141-year run officially ended with the adoption of the euro in 2002.
